content: Cardiac arrest is abrupt loss of heart function, breathing, and consciousness due to electrical or mechanical failure. Out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA) survival ~10% overall; in-hospital ~25%. Chain of Survival: (1) Early recognition and activation of emergency response, (2) Immediate high-quality CPR, (3) Rapid defibrillation for shockable rhythms, (4) Advanced life support and post-arrest care, (5) Recovery including rehabilitation. Initial rhythms: shockable (ventricular fibrillation, pulseless ventricular tachycardia) have better prognosis; non-shockable (asystole, pulseless electrical activity) worse outcomes. Common causes: cardiac (acute MI, cardiomyopathy, arrhythmias), respiratory (hypoxia, PE), metabolic (hyperkalemia, acidosis), hypovolemia, hypothermia, toxins, tamponade, tension pneumothorax ('H's and T's'). Bystander CPR doubles or triples survival. Public access AEDs critical for early defibrillation.

High-quality CPR elements: compression rate 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm (2-2.4 inches) in adults, full chest recoil between compressions, minimize interruptions (<10 seconds), avoid excessive ventilation. Compression-to-ventilation ratio 30:2 for single rescuer; continuous compressions with asynchronous ventilations once advanced airway placed. Hands-only CPR (compressions only) recommended for untrained bystanders—equivalent outcomes for adult sudden collapse. Mechanism: direct cardiac compression and thoracic pump generate blood flow (coronary perfusion pressure critical for ROSC). Feedback devices improve CPR quality. Switch compressors every 2 minutes to prevent fatigue. Defibrillation: biphasic shocks 120-200J (follow device-specific recommendations); resume CPR immediately after shock without rhythm check. Epinephrine 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes improves ROSC and survival to discharge but not neurologically intact survival; give as soon as possible for non-shockable rhythms, after second shock for shockable rhythms. Amiodarone 300mg then 150mg for refractory VF/pVT.

Post-cardiac arrest care: optimize hemodynamics (MAP ≥65 mmHg, often higher needed for cerebral perfusion), oxygenation (SpO2 92-98%, avoid hyperoxia), ventilation (normocapnia, PaCO2 35-45 mmHg), glucose control (140-180 mg/dL). Targeted Temperature Management (TTM): TTM2 trial (2021) showed no difference between 33°C vs strict normothermia with fever prevention in comatose OHCA patients; current guidelines recommend active temperature control with fever prevention (≤37.5°C) for at least 72 hours, target 32-36°C reasonable. Coronary angiography: immediate for STEMI or suspected cardiac cause; delayed/selective for others. Neurologic prognostication: wait ≥72 hours after return to normothermia, use multimodal approach (clinical exam, EEG, SSEP, MRI, biomarkers NSE/S100B). Avoid premature withdrawal of life support. Seizure prophylaxis/treatment common (up to 20% have seizures). Consider ECMO-CPR (extracorporeal CPR) for refractory arrest in specialized centers—EOLIA and other trials show benefit in selected patients. Rehabilitation and psychological support for survivors.
